sentences.text: Good morning.
Frances Gelbart.
Immergluck.
I-M-M-E-R-G-L-U-C-K.
I was born August 20, 1929.
I think it's 66?
[LAUGHS]
I was born in Krakow, Poland.
David.
He used to be part owner of Carp Company, which is transporting fish and supplying for the whole city of Krakow.
He used to, as I remember, travel.
And he knew Mr.
Radziwill, which was, I think, is a father-in-law of Mrs.
Onassis' sisters.
I don't know how you call it, but they used to have big like a pools with fish.
And he used to contract and transport the fish from the area where they used to live to Krakow.
I don't think so, no.
I guess I really don't know.
I thought it was-- from my understanding of today, the standard of living-- it was a nice living.
It was middle upper class.
Very good father.
Well-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --when we saw him, naturally, he worked like fathers work here.
And he traveled quite a bit.
But when we were together, we used to go out together.
We used to go riding together, skiing, skating.
He used to take us to different events, visit families.
It was unfortunately short, but I remember he was very good.
Well, because the war started, and we were caught while we were vacationing.
My father used to come to us for the weekend.
And I remember, as a child, he came one weekend.
He closed the door behind him, with my mother in the room.
And, you know, children are kind of curious.
So my brother-- older brother-- and I, we were curious, and we sort of listened in.
And he was telling mother that he was summoned to the Polish army, that there is a war that we will have to face.
And then he came out, and my mother prepared us that we have to go back home.
That was in about 1939.
Helena Splivinska--
--her maiden name.
She was born also in Poland, Krakow.
She was very young.
She married when she was 16.
So she was very young.
She was very progressive.
She gave us a lot of freedom.
She bought us different type of toys that would stimulate curiosity in our minds.
And then, I come also from a larger family.
So we had like our own kindergarten.
My mother had seven children altogether.
There was Freddie, my older brother.
I was the second, and my name is Frances.
Then there was Eva, Paula, Genya.
There was Henry, the youngest one, that died in a camp.
And then I have a sister Julia, that was born after the war.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Three.
Eva, Paula, and Genya.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think they were nine, seven, and five.
Because we were two years apart from each other.
To that point, it was fun.
I sort of always took charge, because I was the oldest from the girls.
So they looked up to me, and we played together.
We tried to make clothes for the dolls.
And I remember one incident.
One of my sisters took her scissors and went to my mother's closet and cut a piece of one of her beautiful, beautiful dresses, because she loved the material for her doll-- to make a dress.
And I wasn't, I guess, smart enough to stop her.
But I remember we were all punished because what happened.
Still, my mother would never hit us too much, or-- or punished us.
She sort of punished us in a sense-- don't ever, ever go into my closet.
And she gave us the dress to be cut up, because there was no sense of-- of-- of redoing it.
This is one of the incidents I remember.
And I remember going skiing with my brother-- older brother, and skating.
And I felt, and I remember, I had a wonderful childhood.
I used to go to a public school.
And then we had a tutor coming into our house for my brother and me, because we were the oldest one, to teach us French.
And then we also had a rabbi coming to teach us-- pray in Hebrew-- at that time.
No.
No.
I think so.
But I always enjoy more playing, and dancing, and singing, than really studying.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Going skating and being with the family.
We come from a-- a conservative home.
And for Shabbat, my mother always prepared challah-- she baked her own-- and fish, naturally.
We went visiting my grandparents.
My mother always light candles Friday night, which, by the way, I do the same thing.
And the table was surrounded by my family.
And many times we had other part of our extended families to visit us-- when I mean extended, my uncles, and aunts, and cousins.
And I remember when there was a wedding.
There were hardly any outsiders.
Because the family on both sides was so large-- and the cousins, and second cousins, and the children.
Yes, I do.
For high holidays, we used to take up about three or four rows.
Because everybody used to go to the same shul.
And I remember being dressed up beautifully.
And my mother, she was beautiful when she was young.
She's still alive, thank goodness.
And the cousins--
Yes, they did.
The only time I did is when the Germans came to Poland and occupied the city of Krakow.
And you know, they stop us from going to schools.
And I will never forget this.

One of my non-Jewish friends that I used to go to school with, and play with-- and she was also a neighbor from the next building-- when I approached her, she says, I cannot play with you anymore, because you're Jewish.
And I came home crying.
I wanted to play with her, and I couldn't.
Because I guess she was told that she couldn't play with me.
I don't recall my mother's explanation to that.
But that was the beginning of-- what I remember happened afterwards, we had to wear the Star of David bands on the left arms.
Like I mentioned, we couldn't go to schools.
And I remember I couldn't go to theaters, or libraries, or any public places.
I remember-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] --we couldn't walk on the streets.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to-- on the sidewalks, rather.
We had to walk on the streets near the gutters.
We had to be home at a certain time in the evening.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You know, I just was wondering why I couldn't do those things.
But it was explained to me, and actually, there were other Jewish children that were on the same-- in the same situation.
So we sort of played together and accept it as that's the way it has to be.
We also tried to gather.
And-- and we still could get some books and-- and read.
But after we were gathered and pushed into ghettos, we couldn't have books.
I think that was around '41.
I-- I'm not very good at those dates.
I don't know.
I have no idea.
Yes, naturally there were rations at that point.
Before we went to ghetto, we used to get rations.
And I remember standing in a line for bread.
And being that most of the Jewish people wore the Star of David-- and everybody stood in line, Jew and non-Jew.
But sometimes, when you just got to the counter with your number, after standing for hours in a line for the ration, they would push you out.
And you had to go all the way back again, standing in a line.
The result was that by the time you got to the counter, they were out of the rations.
So I know that there was a black market at that time.
And I remember you had to pay dear money.
Everybody was taking advantage of the Jewish people at that time, including the Poles-- not all of them.
Because I remember some of them used to bring food to my mother while we were in ghetto-- some of her friends that she knew.
And actually, that wasn't even easy, because that was punishment by death.
There was always police-- Polish police, German police, standing by the gates.
I am sort of jumping from one subject to the next, but these all relate to--
--what I remember.
I-- I don't remember.
I think was-- we just packed whatever we could.
I didn't take anything.
I never played with dolls.
I used to play more with scales, and I used to be more athletic.
I know I had a bicycle.
Yes, they were like in pharmacy-- old scales with the little weights at that time that they used to make them as toys.
And I guess my mother said I will be a business woman one day.
Because that's what I used to love to play with.
But again, being with my sisters and brothers, and helping my mother, being the oldest daughter-- I was a little mother.
Well, in the ghetto, we lived in this section by name Podgorze.
This is where the ghetto was.
And they concentrated.
I don't know the population of the Jewish people in Krakow at that time, but they pushed them all into maybe five blocks of an area, in this Podgorze ghetto.
And--
--it was-- I'm trying to remember.
I-- I-- I don't remember the name of the street, but I know that my father's family moved in with us.
We had three bedroom apartment.
And I think there must have been about 12 or 15 of us living in this three bedroom apartment.
Our privacy was done by sheets.
And everybody, each bedroom was like a checkerboard square divided by sheets for the families and the older children to have some privacy.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Again, I was helping my mother when we first got in there, in-- in ghetto.
And I made some new Jewish friends from different areas of Krakow.
We used to get together, and we used to sing some Hebrew songs.
Some of the children knew, very well, Hebrew.
And under a penalty of death in ghetto, we were still gathered in a basement with people that were teachers.
And they used to teach us.
They used to--
No, I don't.
Well, were taught to read and write, and arithmetic.
We were taught some Hebrew.
We used to sing some songs in Hebrew.
Shalom Chaverim.
Shalom Chaverim.
I-- I-- we used to fantasize with the girls my own age.
We used to fantasize, when we grow up, what we will do and where we will go.
We used to try to observe the high holidays.
There were-- I remember singing with my father and my sisters, sort of like an entertainment for people.
We used to try to entertain ourselves in ghetto.
There was an orphanage in the ghetto, I remember, where my mother used to take food, whatever we could spare, and share.
And then I remember, also-- I don't how that-- pogrom.
I don't know how you say it in English.
It just escaped me.
They used to come into ghetto with big trucks.
The Germans.
And they used to create a tremendous chaos.
And I remember my father used to come and tell us children, lock the door.
I have to go outside, but you lock the door.
Don't let anybody in, and try to hide.
And then my mother puts us under the beds, behind credenzas.
And we used to sit there for hours.
And we used to hear screaming and shooting outside.
And what was happening, they were with trucks.
And I guess whomever they could catch-- it was like like an animal hunt-- they used to load up in those trucks and take them outside the ghetto.
And the following day, we could hear that-- rather, we never heard from those people.
But it was from the beginning.
And then we heard that they were sent out to some kind of forced labor camps.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that used to happen repeatedly and repeatedly.
And whoever that did not get a identification card that they were somehow employed by the factories that the SS used to own, they were just taken out from the ghetto.
And I, as a very young child, I remember my father somehow-- I don't know how this happened, but he told me one day that, I will be working for a paper factory.
The Jewish people used to own the factory, but the German took over.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And these people used to teach me how to make lines on a tremendous, tremendous printing machine.
I remember I had to be pushed up to stand up on this machine, to feed the tremendous paper so the machine would make lines.
Later on, the papers were cut and sent in to schools, I guess, or to businesses to be sold.
No, I was the youngest.
I was the only child that worked there--
--among adults.
Well, you know, there were ways.
They were-- first of all, they made me look older than what I was.
I had long braids, and they used to put the braids around my head a couple times.
And they used to dress me with a long dresses.
So I appeared, at that time, like 15, not like--
About 11.
And this is how I left ghetto, with this company.
Because they were liquidating ghetto, and they're going to forced labor camp, Plaszow.
And I went with this company.
But before the company was set up there, I was busy washing windows in the barracks.
Because I already was shipped out to Plaszow.
But you could still go out with a group.
We used to go, and come once in awhile to the ghetto.
And I still could see my parents, because my parents were still in ghetto.
They were shipped out with the last transport to Biezanow when the ghetto was liquidated.
But I already was in Plaszow, which is a different camp.
The children went with my parents.
My brother and I went to Plaszow.
But he went to Wieliczka.
Wieliczka, it's another little area, where I guess that they had salt mines.
I really don't know what my brother was doing there, but we were separated.
No, the other four children, because the fifth one was born after the war--
--besides my brother and me.
The other four went with my parents to Biezanow.
OK.
